title_en: Introduction to Al-Bayquniyyah and the Science of Hadith
title_ar: مقدمة في منظومة البيقونية وعلم مصطلح الحديث
content_en:
Al-Manzuma al-Bayquniyyah is a celebrated didactic poem (manzuma) on the science of hadith terminology (mustalah al-hadith), composed by Taha ibn Muhammad al-Bayquni (d. circa 1080 AH / 1669 CE). Despite its brevity — the poem consists of only thirty-four verses — it covers the essential categories of hadith classification with remarkable concision and has become one of the most widely studied introductory texts in the Islamic curriculum. Commentaries (shuruh) on the poem are numerous, and it is typically one of the first texts a student of hadith sciences encounters.

The science of hadith, which al-Bayquniyyah introduces, is one of the most distinctive contributions of the Muslim scholarly tradition to human knowledge. It developed as a systematic discipline for evaluating the chains of transmission (isnad) through which reports about the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) were transmitted across generations. The Muslim scholars recognized early that the reliability of hadith depends entirely on the reliability of the transmitters — that a report, no matter how important its content, is only as trustworthy as the people who transmitted it. This recognition gave rise to a vast apparatus of biographical criticism, known as 'ilm al-rijal or al-jarh wat-ta'dil, which assessed the character, memory, accuracy, and reliability of tens of thousands of transmitters.

Al-Bayquniyyah opens with a verse in which the author praises Allah and sends blessings upon the Prophet, then announces his intention to classify the types of hadith. The poem begins with the most important categories — Sahih (sound), Hasan (good), and Da'if (weak) — which together form the fundamental tripartite classification of hadith by authenticity. This classification is attributed to Imam al-Tirmidhi (d. 279 AH), who was the first to use the term 'hasan' as a technical category distinct from both sahih and da'if, and who formalized the three-tier system that subsequent hadith scholarship adopted.

The Sahih hadith is the highest category. Al-Bayquniyyah defines it as a hadith that has a continuous chain (muttasil) of reliable (thiqat) transmitters, without any break (inqita') or defect ('illa), and without being shudh (irregular, meaning contradicting stronger evidence). Each transmitter in the chain must satisfy the conditions of 'adala (moral uprightness and religious reliability) and dabt (precision and accuracy in transmission, whether by memory or by written text). When all these conditions are met across every link of the chain, from the final compiler to the Prophet himself, the hadith is classified as Sahih and may be used as evidence in legal and theological matters.

The Hasan hadith occupies an intermediate position. It meets most of the conditions of sahih — its chain is continuous, its narrators are morally upright — but one or more of its transmitters is of lesser precision than required for the highest grade. The hadith is still reliable enough to be acted upon and to constitute legal evidence, but it does not reach the highest level of certainty. The concept of hasan was developed to avoid a binary classification that would leave a large number of reliable but imperfect reports without a recognized status. Imam al-Tirmidhi's introduction of hasan as a formal category represented a significant jurisprudential insight: that degrees of probability in historical evidence should be reflected in degrees of evidential weight.

The Da'if hadith is any hadith that fails to meet the conditions of hasan or sahih, whether due to a broken chain, an unreliable narrator, a concealed defect, or some other problem. The study of commentaries on al-Bayquniyyah reveals the rich diversity of da'if subtypes — mawdu' (fabricated), munkar (rejected), shadhdh (irregular), mu'allal (subtly defective), and many others — each with its own definition and evidentiary implications. A da'if hadith is generally not used as evidence in legal rulings, though scholars have debated whether it may be cited in contexts of spiritual encouragement (fada'il al-a'mal) when no stronger evidence is available.

The study of al-Bayquniyyah and its commentaries thus introduces the student to a sophisticated epistemological tradition: a systematic approach to evaluating historical claims that balances the need for religious evidence with rigorous standards of verification. The scholars of hadith developed this tradition over several centuries, producing an achievement unique in the history of pre-modern scholarship — a comprehensive critical apparatus for assessing the authenticity of a vast body of reports from a single individual across hundreds of years of oral and written transmission.
content_ar: البيقونية منظومة مختصرة جامعة لأنواع علم الحديث، ابتدأها صاحبها بالصحيح والحسن والضعيف.
